speaker: interviewee
text: Obviously it was taken in winter. She seems to be well-protected.

speaker: interviewer
text: She's got a doll in her hands.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: In, in [CZECH] which is near Carlsbad in northern Czechoslovakia.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. And what date are we looking at, approximately?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think, I think she would have been about four years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right, and she was born--

speaker: interviewee
text: She was born in '97. So it would have been around the turn of 1900, 1901, something like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Edith, who's in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: Seems to be an atelier photograph of my mother and her younger brother. And I think the lady in the middle is her aunt. They must have been visiting for holidays.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old was your mother at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: I should think about 10 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. So this was taken--

speaker: interviewee
text: 1907, something like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: And whereabouts?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think it was probably taken in Theresienstadt.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. So that was a place they went for holidays.

speaker: interviewee
text: They went for holiday. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Not knowing what would be in store.

speaker: interviewee
text: --In store, exactly. My aunts-- her-- the aunts lived, two maiden aunts lived in Theresienstadt. And my mother used to go there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was it a pretty place?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: No. So not somewhere you would have chosen for a holiday.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. But it was cheap if you stayed with your aunt.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right, OK. Edith, who's in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: My grandfather-- paternal grandfather-- grandmother, and my father on the right. My uncle and aunt next to my grandmother. I should think my father would have been about two years at that stage.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was the picture taken.

speaker: interviewee
text: That was also in Theresienstadt. [LAUGHS] Because my father's family lived there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when he was a child, he--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, that was taken actually in Litomerice, which is the bigger town, next to Theresienstadt.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: Over the river.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right, thank you. Edith, who's in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: It is a picture of my father and his friends. My father was an artillery officer on the Italian front. And this was taken in December, 1917. My father is in the middle.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. And how old would he have been about in this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Would have been 21-- 1917, 23, 23.

speaker: interviewer
text: And is this the Austro-Prussian army?

speaker: interviewee
text: Austro-- not Prussian. Austro-Hungarian.

speaker: interviewer
text: Austro-Hungarian army, right. And so did he serve for the full length of the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, oh, yes. He served.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was quite lucky that he survived it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, he was wounded a couple of times. But he survived.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you know where this picture was taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: Somewhere-- I think it would have been in the mountains in Italy, I should imagine. In the Dolomites. He fought in the Dolomites. And he lost a bit of his hearing because of the artillery being very noisy. And I think it should be-- would be there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right, thank you. Who's in this picture, Edith?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's after my father and his friend came back from the wars. It is in my grandmother's house, in Theresienstadt. My father is the second from the left. My two aunts are on the outside, in the middle is a cousin of theirs. And the fellow on the other side was my father's best friend.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so what date are we looking at?

speaker: interviewee
text: I should imagine about '20, '21.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. And where would the picture have been taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Theresienstadt.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. OK, Edith, who's in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's the whole Drucker family. I don't know what the occasion was, probably somebody's birthday, I should imagine. In the middle is my grandfather, my grandmother. And there's an old aunt to my grandmother's side. On the other side is my grandmother's unmarried sister. In the forefront is some cousin who I can't remember. And my uncles and aunts and my father and mother-- third and fourth from the left in the background.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when do you think this picture was taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: I should imagine about '24, 1924.

speaker: interviewer
text: And whereabout?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Theresienstadt again. It was in the backyard of my grandmother's shop.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what uniform is your one uncle wearing?

speaker: interviewee
text: My uncle was a major in the Czech army. He was the non-Jewish part of the family. And he was the one who protected his wife very courageously, refusing to divorce her.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was something which some people did.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Because if they-- once they divorced them, they lost-- they became nonpersons, and they were deported. Whereas if they were not divorced, they were protected, right till the end. She finished in Theresienstadt in-- I think-- in February '45. But that was only for three months. And all these mixed marriages were protected from transports.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. And who have we got here, Edith?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's the wedding-- seems to be the wedding picture of my aunt Marta. She's in the middle, with her husband, Gustav. My father and my mother are to one side. And my other aunt and probably her suitor at the time to the other side.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what year are we talking about?

speaker: interviewee
text: I should think it would be about '24, also, 1924.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Theresienstadt?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Theresienstadt, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: How far in distance was Theresienstadt from--

speaker: interviewee
text: Prague? About 60K. 60 kilometers.

speaker: interviewer
text: So very close. I mean, almost like a suburb.

words.text: I mean, almost like a suburb.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, fairly close. Well, today it's close. In the olden days, it was quite a long way.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was still far, right. And how would you have traveled, a train?

speaker: interviewee
text: By train.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Who is in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father, and my mother on the right. It's taken in 1924. The two unidentified bathing belles on the left, I don't know who they are. I think it's on the river in Theresienstadt again.

speaker: interviewer
text: They're in their swimsuits.

speaker: interviewee
text: Their swimsuits, very immoral.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Who do we have in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father, my mother, and myself. I should think it was in 1927, after my mother brought me back from hospital. It's taken in our bedroom. I can recognize the curtains.

speaker: interviewer
text: Beautiful, thank you. Edith, who's in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's my mother and myself. I was, I should think about seven years old then. I had a frock on which I hated. It was itchy. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was that?

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old would your mother have been?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was about seven. She would have been 36.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Who's in this picture, Edith?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's again my mother and myself. I think it would have been in 1938. I should imagine I was about 11. My mother already looks very sad. And I think it was already the Hitler time coming. I think it was about that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Edith, who's in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's me. It's at Bobbin Head. And that was the first excursion I took in Australia. It was a big bushwalk. And I was absolutely flabbergasted how beautiful it was and all the boats and all the water.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was about 1950?

speaker: interviewee
text: '49, I think.

speaker: interviewer
text: '49, Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: I think it was shortly after I arrived.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, thank you. Do you-- Edith, who's in that picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's myself again. I think it was taken about the same time as the one in the checkered frock with the big white collar.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: I had to get undressed. It was pretty cold. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: So this was to be a sophisticated portrait.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. [LAUGHTER] I hated it. It was obviously in springtime, because there were snowdrops, real snowdrops there on the cloth.

speaker: interviewer
text: Beautiful portrait. Thank you. Edith, who's in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: Myself again. I think it was 1934. I would have been seven or eight years old then.

speaker: interviewer
text: And taken where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In a, in Prague.

speaker: interviewer
text: Prague, OK, thank you. Edith, who's in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: Myself sitting on a donkey. It's a portrait of two donkeys, as my mother used to say. And it was in Luhacovice, which is a spa in Moravia, where I used to have respiratory troubles, and I had to drink this spa water, which I always used to dribble down onto the footpath. I never drank it. And the cure didn't take.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who's in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's a picture of my maternal grandmother, Louisa Auerbach. She was-- she would have been in her late 60s by then, I think. She lived with us and looked after me. I was very, very devoted to her. She was a wonderful person.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. OK, who's in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's myself, four months before going into camp, 1942. I was 15 then.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Edith, who's in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's myself again, at the time of my matriculation. It was a habit in Prague that the whole class had photos taken, which were mounted on a big board-- also the photos of the teachers-- and exhibited in some prominent shop. It was called the tableau. And then one used to go around and have a look at all the other people's tableau from different schools.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they took beautiful portraits in those days, beautiful. Thank you. Edith, who's this picture of? Which place?

speaker: interviewee
text: That is just outside Theresienstadt. It's the bridge over the river. We can see the mill there. It's obviously in springtime, when the-- a lot of flotsam and jetsam comes down the river. On-- we had to cross that bridge always-- there were always controls at the head, going back. We had to cross that bridge going to work and from work, when I worked in the fields. And you can see the fortifications of Theresienstadt already behind the bridge. And in the far distance, you can see the middle mountains, which are just over the Sudeten border.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Edith, which place is this?

speaker: interviewee
text: We're looking at Theresienstadt, after the war, in 1970. That was the central square. In the middle, you can see the church. On the right was the barracks where I stayed. On the left are the barracks, which were initially the German Kommandatura, later on the German girls barracks. The steeple had a clock where a watchmaker had to go up every day and wind the clock, right through the time of the camp. So that was our only way of knowing what time it was.

speaker: interviewer
text: Amazing. Your watches were confiscated?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Who's in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother with my two sons. It's 1964. Peter, the older, on the right. Stephen in the middle. They're all making funny faces.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Sydney.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Edith, who's in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was during the Children of the Holocaust exhibition in the War Memorial in Melbourne. We were on the panel. That's John Glass and myself. And there was a question time. And we were just answering some questions, put to us by the public.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. And this one?

speaker: interviewee
text: Is also at the War Memorial. It's in the guides room, with the other voluntary guides from the War Memorial in Canberra.

speaker: interviewer
text: At the time of the exhibition?

speaker: interviewee
text: At the time of the exhibition, that's right.

speaker: interviewer
text: So were you a guide for the exhibition?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I was a guide for the exhibition as well.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you stayed there for a while.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we just went down in the morning and went back in the evening or stayed overnight, whichever.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. This picture of?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's our family, before the boys married. It's on our back veranda. And we obviously just had a meal. But I don't know when it is exactly. It would be about 10 years ago.

speaker: interviewer
text: About '85?

speaker: interviewee
text: Something like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: And which son is on the left?

speaker: interviewee
text: On the left is Stephen, the younger. Peter is on the right.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your husband on your right?

speaker: interviewee
text: My husband on my right, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And his name is?

speaker: interviewee
text: Walter.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Who is this picture of?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was of my husband and myself. My son Peter, who was already at the Griffith University at Brisbane, invited us up for a weekend to a holiday resort. And we had a lovely time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your husband change his name when he got to Australia?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes. He was Schuessler before. But it was just too difficult. And his brother changed it first, so he conformed and changed it also.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Edith, who's in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: Two convicts, Walter and Edith. We went to Norfolk Island with the [INAUDIBLE] on a bridge holiday. And the last night was a dinner dance. And we were all dressed in convict suits. So it was a wonderful time.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was fun.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you work during your years that you were-- your children were growing up?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes, I was working all the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: What area?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was first a secretary, then as a bookkeeper. When I got too old for the front office, I went to the back office. [LAUGHTER] Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK Edith, who's in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: On the left is my older grandson Danny.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: He would have been about two at the time. And obviously, he didn't like something on the menu. On the right hand side is my younger grandson. He would have been not quite a year then. He's called Leon and lives in Sydney.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Who's in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was taken, I think, for my birthday last year. My older son Peter, his wife Louise, and Danny. And he seems to be happy this time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Good. And how old is he now?

speaker: interviewee
text: He's just over two, two and a quarter.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. And they live in Sydney?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they live-- that's the ones that live in--

speaker: interviewer
text: Brisbane.

speaker: interviewee
text: Brisbane. My daughter-in-law is also a lecturer at the Griffith University.

speaker: interviewer
text: What subject?

speaker: interviewee
text: Same thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Industrial relations.

speaker: interviewee
text: Industrial relations, yes. They already knew each other from Sydney University. And then they met by accident again in Queensland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Amazing. Thank you. Who's in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: My younger son and his wife Marianne. And this seems to be Leon, as a much younger child. He seems to be quite a baby. And it's in Gordon's Bay in Coogee.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.
